era_context: The early 11th century continues with the Song Dynasty in China, the Fatimid Caliphate in the Islamic world, the Ghaznavid Empire under Mahmud at its zenith, and Western Europe in the early feudal period. This is a time of significant cultural and technological advancement, religious ferment, military competition, and the expansion of long-distance trade networks. The period is marked by the growth of monastic institutions, the development of feudal hierarchies, and the beginning of the Crusade movement's ideological foundations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no Old World written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe beyond Rus' and Scandinavia
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse documentation)

events.title: Accession of Conrad II as King of the Romans (Holy Roman Emperor-elect)
events.description: Conrad II (Conrad the Salian) was elected King of the Romans in 1024 and formally crowned in 1026. This election marked the beginning of the Salian dynasty's rule in the Holy Roman Empire and represented a significant shift in imperial politics. Conrad II was the first king of the Salian line and sought to strengthen imperial authority against the nobility and the papacy. His election and coronation were contested by some German princes, but he ultimately consolidated his power and became one of the most important Holy Roman Emperors of the 11th century.
key_figures: Henry II (previous Holy Roman Emperor, d. 1024)
events.certainty_note: Conrad II's election and coronation as King of the Romans in 1024–1026 is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: Precedes Conrad II's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in Rome (1027)
Part of the Salian dynasty's rise to power

events.title: Rajendra I's naval expedition to the Srivijaya Empire
events.region: South Asia and Southeast Asia / Chola Empire and Srivijaya
events.description: Rajendra I (Chola Emperor, r. 1014–1044) launched a major naval expedition against the Srivijaya Empire in Southeast Asia, extending Chola military power across the Indian Ocean. This expedition, which may have occurred in 1025–1026 or slightly later, resulted in the conquest of several Srivijaya territories and the establishment of Chola naval dominance in the region. The expedition demonstrated the Chola Empire's sophisticated maritime capabilities and its ambitions to control Indian Ocean trade routes. The conquest was commemorated in Chola inscriptions and represented the height of Chola imperial power.

sources.name: Chola inscriptions (epigraphic evidence), particularly the Tanjore inscription
Archaeological evidence from Southeast Asian sites

events.certainty_note: Rajendra I's naval expedition is well-attested in Chola inscriptions, but the precise date is uncertain. Sources place it between 1025 and 1030 CE. The expedition itself is not disputed, but the exact year is approximate.
cross_references: Part of Rajendra I's broader program of military expansion
Contemporary with the completion of the Brihadesvara Temple (c. 1028)
Demonstrates Chola maritime dominance in the Indian Ocean

events.title: Caliph al-Zahir's continued reign and administrative consolidation
events.description: Caliph al-Zahir li-A'izam Allah (r. 1021–1036) continued his reign over the Fatimid Caliphate, consolidating administrative control and managing the complex political situation in the Levant and North Africa. Al-Zahir's reign was marked by efforts to restore stability after the destructive reign of his predecessor al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (996–1021). Al-Zahir faced challenges from the Bedouin tribes, the Nizari Ismaili movement, and external military threats from the Byzantine Empire and the Ghaznavid Empire. His reign represented a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing in the Fatimid Caliphate.
key_figures: Caliph al-Zahir li-A'izam Allah (Fatimid Caliph, r. 1021–1036)

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa bi-Akhbar al-A'imma al-Fatimiyyin (History of the Fatimid Caliphs), written 15th century
Ibn al-Athir, Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh (Complete History), written 12th–13th century

events.certainty_note: Al-Zahir's reign is well-attested in Islamic sources, but specific events of 1026 are not clearly documented. The year is part of a broader period of administrative consolidation.
cross_references: Contemporary with Ghaznavid Empire under Mahmud
Precedes the Seljuk rise to power (1040s)

disconfirming_evidence: The precise date of Rajendra I's naval expedition is uncertain; sources place it between 1025 and 1030 CE, with some scholars arguing for a date closer to 1030. The expedition's extent and significance are also debated; some scholars argue that Chola control over Srivijaya territories was limited and temporary. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of Conrad II's accession or al-Zahir's reign, but precision should not be overstated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1026 is moderate but regionally uneven. Western European sources provide good coverage of Conrad II's political rise. South Asian sources (epigraphic evidence and later Tamil literature) document Rajendra I's military campaigns, though dating is approximate. Islamic sources document al-Zahir's reign, though specific events of 1026 are not clearly marked. Byzantine sources are available but less detailed for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe remain poorly documented. The surviving record privileges elite political and military events over economic and social history.

graph_edges.note: Conrad II's election as King of the Romans in 1024–1026 led to his coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in 1027
The naval expedition is part of Rajendra I's broader program of military and maritime expansion
Al-Zahir's administrative consolidation occurred during a period of relative stability that preceded the Seljuk rise to power
